Australian demolition derby – a high-octane version of dodgem cars

# Australia’s demolition derby collides with safety rules as fans fight to save the ‘oddball’ motor sport

Pitting beaten-up cars against each other at rural agricultural shows, the sport has been in the spotlight since a crash last year that injured spectators

- Get our [breaking news email](https://www.theguardian.com/email-newsletters?CMP=cvau_sfl), [free app](https://app.adjust.com/w4u7jx3) or [daily news podcast](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/series/full-story?CMP=cvau_sfl)


The demolition derby is an “oddball” sport, its enthusiasts say. Beaten up cars crash into one another on mud-soaked fields at rural agricultural shows, thrilling country crowds in a high octane version of dodgem cars.

Now, the Australian demolition derby community is fighting to keep the sport alive in the face of new safety rules it claims are heaping extra costs on to organisers and threatening its survival unless a compromise can be reached.

The motor sport has been in the safety spotlight since a car went through a barrier at the [Walcha Motorcycle Rally in November](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2025/nov/16/thirteen-people-injured-after-speedway-car-ploughs-into-grandstand-during-nsw-demolition-derby) last year, injuring 13 people and leaving two in a critical condition.

After the incident, SafeWork [New South Wales](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/new-south-wales), in conjunction with AgShows NSW – the body representing agricultural shows across the state – amended operational guidelines for derby events to include appropriate safety barrier types and arena designs.

![Aussie demolition derby 1 – loop](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/uploads/2026/03/25/Aussie_demolition_derby_1___loop--ad9b4b30-694f-495a-96c6-0785f386d6b3-3.0.0000000.jpg?width=120&dpr=1&s=none&crop=16%3A9)

Aussie demolition derby 1 – loop

The changes have sent the industry into a spin, with many claiming the cost of compliance to be unsustainable.

The Australian Demolition Derby Association (ADDA) president, Nathan Williams, says the changes have badly affected the sport.

[![A helicopter lands on a sports field at night](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/0a955291d7be4941620e6feb3caa552e6481d2ce/304_0_1440_1153/master/1440.jpg?width=460&quality=85&auto=format&fit=max&s=dc39837c1eee8bae69fbf0e0b2c2f2dc)\\
\\
Thirteen people injured after demolition derby car ploughs into grandstand during NSW event \\
\\
Read more](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2025/nov/16/thirteen-people-injured-after-speedway-car-ploughs-into-grandstand-during-nsw-demolition-derby)

“The decision is massive; there have already been five derbies in NSW … that have been cancelled … We’ve lost two of our events in [Queensland](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/queensland) due to just the ruling; they don’t want to be a part of it with the water barrier situation,” he says.

Despite its good-natured violence, it has been many years since the sport has caused major controversy. The first-ever demolition derby was held in Australia in February 1964 at Rowley Park Speedway in Adelaide, attracting the largest crowd ever seen at the track.

More than 20,000 people stormed the gates, with many more patrons stranded outside.

The chaotic event was abandoned after only 37 minutes as children swarmed across the track in excitement.

Williams agrees the incident in Walcha should never have happened, but emphasises it was the first occurence of its kind in five decades.

The new regulations stipulate the use of water-filled barriers made of high density polyethylene plastic containers, which when filled create an energy-absorbing heavy fence able to withstand crashes from vehicles travelling up to 70km/h.

Williams says the barriers had cost a derby in Maitland up to $15,000 – an additional expense that had led to the cancellation of at least seven events in NSW and Queensland this season, which ends this month.

![Aussie demolition derby 3 – loop](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/uploads/2026/03/25/Aussie_demolition_derby_3___loop--5a311fea-3662-4497-b090-57d2e86ec263-1.0.0000000.jpg?width=120&dpr=1&s=none&crop=16%3A9)

Aussie demolition derby 3 – loop

One of the cancelled derbies was to take place at the Cessnock show. The president of the Cessnock and District Agricultural Association, Shiralee Adams, says the impact of cancelling the derby is affecting show ticket sales.

“We only had about a quarter of the public come through our gate,” she says, describing the derby as a “huge drawcard” to rural shows.

[![Eastern Creek Sydney Dragway, NSW, Australia](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/dd49cce1fe1e3f96723260680925b5319a49713a/0_518_6179_4946/master/6179.jpg?width=460&quality=85&auto=format&fit=max&s=0bcdcef8f69d6d6f5b51eea41f4e6832)\\
\\
Drag racing at Sydney’s weekly street meet offers a change of pace for all walks of life\\
\\
Read more](https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2026/jan/22/sydney-drag-racing-weekly-street-meet)

She says it took from 11 at night to 5.30 in the morning to install and fill the water barriers in the Maitland arena alone. “We can’t justify $13,000 to hire those for the Cessnock show,” she says.

She also raises concerns about SafeWorks’ proposed plan for a singular entry point into the arena, given the passage could be blocked by a broken-down car or hinder emergency access.

![Demolition derby cars in Gloucester NSW.](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/3bd36963379544de84daaf8096602097b4507c8a/0_0_1536_2048/master/1536.jpg?width=445&dpr=1&s=none&crop=none)[View image in fullscreen](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2026/apr/11/australias-demolition-derby-collides-with-safety-rules-as-fans-fight-to-save-the-oddball-motor-sport#img-1)

Demolition derby cars in Gloucester, NSW. Photograph: Australian Demolition Derby Association

“What happens if we have an incident happen over the far side of the track … how do we get emergency personnel in to either put a fire out or to treat an injured driver?

“They said, ‘Oh, you’ve got to move the barriers … You need about a 10-tonne forklift.”

“We’re an ag show. We have a 70-horsepower tractor with a set of forks. We can’t physically move them … Your rules to keep our public safe … are putting our drivers in jeopardy,” Adams said.

Adams is determined to revive the demolition derby next year and has already started talks with SafeWork about how to make it achievable.

Williams says the ADDA has tried to work with SafeWork since the Walcha incident, but has been unable to come to an agreement about barrier regulations.

“We’ve tried to communicate with them to come up with a safe plan, a better or more affordable plan, but they don’t want to hear it,” he says.

As a minimum safety requirement, the association laid telegraph poles placed on their side as barriers and offered to stack half-tonne hay bales and tractor tyres along the trackside.

Williams says SafeWork verbally agreed to the measures but was unwilling to put anything in writing.

A spokesperson for SafeWork says: “Demolition Derby event holders are required to conduct risk assessments and implement adequate control measures when planning these events, taking into account the nature of the planned activity and the venue design.”

![Demolition derby car at Gloucester NSW](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/2753a7fffa163ad81964b6830cd66272f5ec2d9e/0_532_1536_938/master/1536.jpg?width=445&dpr=1&s=none&crop=none)[View image in fullscreen](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2026/apr/11/australias-demolition-derby-collides-with-safety-rules-as-fans-fight-to-save-the-oddball-motor-sport#img-2)

‘As much as we love destroying each other on the field, before and after it we’re just a big family,’ the Australian Demolition Derby Association president says. Photograph: Australian Demolition Derby Association

Williams says every event is fully insured and the ADDA’s insurers accept their plan and set-up.

The ADDA is made up of about 30 members, who describe it as a tight-knit family, with the children and friends of drivers regularly attending the events.

“As much as we love destroying each other on the field, before and after it we’re just a big family. Everyone helps everyone,” Williams says.

He worries the industry will disappear if the sport is unable to come to an agreement with SafeWork soon. Derbies are not run for profit, most prize pools barely cover the cost of the cars – and safety modifications are made out-of-pocket.

He says: “The attraction of the sport is that it is one-of-a-kind, an absolute oddball … that doesn’t make sense to a lot of people, but they damn sure want to give it a go.”

Tenille Fisher grew up watching her dad and older brother compete across regional Australia before becoming a driver herself. She now watches her husband and two younger brothers regularly compete – but is concerned for the future of the sport.

“We’re already seeing events cancelled, and with the costs and uncertainty around requirements, it’s becoming harder for organisers and drivers to keep things running. Everyone is trying their best to accommodate the rules, but it’s difficult when they change from event to event,” she says.

She has competed in the women’s derbies at Taree, Wingham and Dungog, and says she hopes her children will one day compete.

“Everyone supports improving safety, especially after what’s happened, but the way SafeWork NSW is implementing the changes has been really difficult,” she says, while also acknowledging how supportive the derby community is.

“What I love most is the atmosphere and the community; it really does feel like an extended family.”

AgShow NSW has declined to comment.

# The Iran war prompted a cut to the fuel excise, so why is diesel so expensive?

By business reporters [Adelaide Miller](https://www.abc.net.au/news/adelaide-miller/102482222) and [Emilia Terzon](https://www.abc.net.au/news/emilia-terzon/5769642)

- [Topic:Petrol Prices](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/petrol-prices)

4h ago4 hours agoSat 11 Apr 2026 at 3:04pm

![Petrol punps at a service station.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e682a7bde7e87db1706dc98378938451?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1440&cropW=2560&xPos=0&yPos=133&width=862&height=485)

The price of diesel continues to soar, despite government measures to decrease the cost. (ABC News: Jasmine Hughes)

The price of diesel continues to soar as the war in the Middle East rages on and access to global fuel supplies remains restricted.

Despite the federal government cutting the fuel tax excise, the average price of diesel is well above $3 a litre again.

Experts and industry advocates warn this will put further pressure on inflation and force some road transport operators to close their doors.

But why is diesel rising while petrol is falling? And what impact does it have on Australia, given most freight transport relies on diesel, along with almost 30 per cent of passenger vehicles?

## Why is diesel so much more expensive than petrol?

While the majority of those driving a petrol car have the flexibility to change their modes of transport during a crisis, many of those driving diesel-powered commercial vehicles often do not.

Diesel prices dropped by a smaller amount than unleaded when the excise was cut

That is because 90 per cent of everyday goods are [transported by road](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-10/rising-oil-prices-impacting-already-struggling-truck-industry/106381214), using mainly diesel-powered vehicles.

"For unleaded, higher prices can sometimes lead households to cut back on driving or change their behaviour, which helps ease demand,"  says Kushneel Prakash from the Melbourne Institute of Applied Economics and Social Research.

"But diesel is used heavily in freight, mining, agriculture and commercial transport, where there is far less room to reduce usage.

"That combination of tighter supply and less flexible demand is why diesel is surging while unleaded has remained relatively more stable, despite the cut in excise taxes in Australia."

Nevertheless, industry advocates are finding diesel prices at the bowser concerning.

"It's always been a bit mysterious, hasn't it, how the fuel price rises and falls at the bowser? We're all very suspicious about it and this industry is very suspicious about it," Transport Workers' Union national secretary Michael Kaine says.

He says the price of diesel has doubled since February.

The increase has led to the competition watchdog [investigating allegations of anti-competitive conduct by major fuel suppliers](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-19/accc-takes-enforcement-action-fuel-suppliers/106472070) in regional and rural Australia, including Ampol, BP, Mobil Oil, and Viva Energy.

## Hang on. I thought the fuel excise was cut?

Yes. The federal government agreed to [halve the fuel excise on petrol and diesel by 26.3 cents a litre](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-30/fuel-excise-halved-three-months-petrol-diesel/106510432) in an effort to moderate rising prices.

This was then [topped up by premiers and chief ministers from around the nation](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-02/further-fuel-excise-cut-from-gst-revenue/106525678), cutting an extra 5.7 cents per litre, bringing the total reduction to 32 cents per litre until June 30.

But less than two weeks later, the price is soaring again.

As of Friday, the NRMA said the average price for diesel across the country was 323 cents per litre.

![A man with a salt-and-pepper beard sits behind computer screens in an office.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d0237f6e832aa0a164a84c82893b6bbf?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Phil Bullock says supply disruptions are disproportionately affecting diesel. (ABC News: John Gunn)

But it is also important to note that petrol and diesel prices are set using different benchmarks.

"While crude oil prices have eased slightly, Australian fuel prices are set off international refined-fuel benchmarks, not crude," NineSquared director Phil Bullock says.

His firm specialises in economics and public policy consulting, with a focus on transport and freight, for the government and private sector.

"Diesel is priced off the Singapore gasoil benchmark, which has risen far more than petrol benchmarks and remains elevated," Mr Bullock says.

One reason for the increase, he says, is because 60-70 per cent of crude oil processed by Singapore’s refineries comes from the Middle East.

When Singaporean refineries do access oil from other parts of the world, Mr Bullock says they will also likely incur additional transport costs.

"Global supply disruptions and the structure of refining in Asia have disproportionately affected diesel, keeping international diesel prices high despite recent fuel excise reductions," he says.

## What could the government do to help bring prices down?

Some experts say the government should have prioritised a larger cut to the diesel excise rather than cutting the petrol excise.

![A smiling man in a dark suit stands outside a building.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2f1f6984762a8001d48c8e6701d6a874?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=742&cropW=1113&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Lurion De Mello says the cost of diesel will affect inflation. (Supplied: Macquarie University)

“I would have expected the government to cut the fuel excise on diesel a lot more than what they did with petrol, because … diesel is a big problem," says Macquarie University Lurion De Mello, who specialises in economics and finance.

Higher diesel costs impact inflation more than petrol because diesel-powered trucks move pretty much everything we buy.

"Diesel is going to have a huge inflationary impact on our groceries, transportation, things that we import from overseas as well," Dr De Mello says.

![Bowsers at a petrol station.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/629e150458fe4dbc39fbcad251866dc3?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=4672&cropW=7008&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Diesel prices pose a bigger problem than petrol, according to Lurion De Mello. (ABC News: Chris Taylor)

He pointed to other countries, including India, [that have reduced their taxes on diesel](https://www.reuters.com/world/india/india-cuts-special-additional-excise-duties-petrol-diesel-2026-03-27/) to almost nothing to help industry and farmers.

As it stands, [inflation rose 3.7 per cent in February, down 0.1 per cent from the previous month](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-25/inflation-fell-slightly-in-february-underlying-figure-unchanged/106494960), but remains above the Reserve Bank's target band and does not yet capture the impact of the global energy crisis that took hold in March.

The chief executive of Westpac has warned of the [growing risk of a recession](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-03/westpac-chief-executive-andrew-miller-recession-interest-rates/106525336) for Australia and the bank's chief economist predicts three more rate hikes this year because of the energy crisis.

"The reality is the conflict in the Middle East has raised energy prices and the pass-through of that increase in fuel prices to other prices in the Australian economy has come through quite quickly and in large measure," chief economist Luci Ellis says.

## What’s next for diesel prices?

It's going to get worse before it gets better, according to energy analyst Saul Kavonic, who says "the real shortage hasn't hit us yet".

![A dark-haired man in a dark blazer types on a laptop while sitting at  a desk in an office.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1b3b229a0843672ba8848375e1a0cdeb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=276&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Saul Kavonic says the worst of the energy crisis is yet to come. (ABC News)

"We're still living off the fuel that left the Strait of Hormuz before the war began."

He says the "real crunch-point" will come at the end of the month and in May.

"Then we'll start to see increased competition from other nations in Asia and globally, who will all be trying to scramble and secure the last little bits of fuel that are left," he says.

"There is no doubt whatsoever that making sure we have availability of fuel is absolutely critical for not just our energy security, but our economic welfare and, frankly, for our national security."

![A middle-aged man with short grey hair wears a suit while standing on a city street.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/111196aae6f67014b31ab15acf4c1d49?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2252&cropW=3378&xPos=562&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Michael Kaine has been at the Fair Work Commission to call for major operators to share the energy price burden. (ABC News: Thorsten Joses)

Mr Kaine says many trucking companies are at breaking point.

"Those at the very top of supply chains, those reaping the economic benefit are … the major retailers, manufacturers, miners," he says.

"What they do is, they essentially impose whatever they want in contract terms and conditions on the industry."

Mr Kaine and other road transport stakeholders were at the Fair Work Commission this week, [calling for retailers, manufacturers and mining companies to share the burden of rising energy prices](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-08/transport-industry-fair-work-commission-fuel-crisis-drivers-help/106540708).

"If we don't get that right, we're going to see our supermarket shelves empty, we're going to see medical supplies slow down, we'll see the wheels of transport come to a standstill and of course that is not what we need in our community," he says.

# Second midwife charged with manslaughter after failed Newcastle homebirth

1d
•

1 min read

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

A second woman has been charged with manslaughter over the death of a baby after a failed home birth in Newcastle.

Police allege private midwife Jordan Michaela Holland, 28, attended a Wallsend home on October 2, 2024, to assist with a home birth.

Ms Holland has been charged with manslaughter over the newborn's death.

She fronted Newcastle bail court today and was released on strict conditions.

Police allege the attempted home birth spanned two days, and the mother's requests to be transferred to hospital were ignored despite complications.

Police documents tendered in court as part of the bail application process said the mother attended the John Hunter Hospital on October 4, 2024.

The baby boy was delivered by emergency caesarean on that day, but died in hospital on October 10, 2024.

Strike Force Girona was established to investigate the baby's death.

#### Bail granted

Judge James Viney today approved Ms Holland's release with conditions, including that she not engage in the care, education, nursing or midwifery of any person.

Ms Holland is also banned from contacting any prosecution witness and prohibited from applying for a passport.

She must report to Maitland Police Station once a week.

A second midwife, Oyebola Coxon, 36, has also been charged in relation to the baby's death.

Ms Coxon was charged with manslaughter and recklessly causing grievous bodily harm in August 2025.

She is also on bail and due back in court for a committal hearing on April 15.

# Thousands across New Zealand preparing to evacuate as Cyclone Vaianu closes in

Story by Joseph Sahyoun

• 21h
•

2 min read

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Thousands of people across [New Zealand](https://www.9news.com.au/new-zealand "New Zealand") are being prepared to evacuate as [Cyclone](https://www.9news.com.au/cyclone "Cyclone") Vaianu moves closer to the nation, bringing severe [weather](https://www.9news.com.au/weather-news "weather") conditions.

The system is set to reach New Zealand by Saturday night, with a state of emergency being declared ﻿for Northland, Whakatāne and Hawke's Bay as they prepare for what is being called a "major wind event".

Multiple [emergency](https://www.9news.com.au/emergencies "emergency") alerts have been sent out today across New Zealand, including in Auckland, urging residents ﻿to brace for [heavy rain](https://www.9news.com.au/rain "heavy rain") and strong [wind warnings](https://www.9news.com.au/storms "wind warnings").

**READ MORE:** [**Police appeal for information after Victorian man, 65, fatally shot and buried in state's north-west﻿**](https://www.9news.com.au/national/police-appeal-for-information-following-suspicious-death-in-ouyen/6d33322f-6657-40ac-863b-d2e958b081d8 "Police appeal for information after Victorian man, 65, fatally shot and buried in state's north-west﻿")

"Prepare now. Have a plan to shelter at home, deal with potential power outages, or evacuate if necessary," the alert said.

"Follow forecasts and do not wait for official warnings.

"Wind damage, flooding and landslides can occur at any time."﻿

_Stuff﻿_ has reported that the entire North Island is under both severe weather watches and warnings, with strong wind warnings for Great Barrier Island and the Coromandel Peninsula, where gusts of up to 130km/h are expected.

The nation's South Island is reportedly bracing for heavy rain across ﻿eastern Marlborough, especially between Blenheim and Kaikōura and near the Richmond Range.

**LIVE:** [**Why peace talks are going on without Donald Trump﻿**](https://www.9news.com.au/world/us-israel-iran-war-live-updates-april-11-donald-trump-latest-world-news-headlines/7ab10193-c125-4c2f-8cba-7c5bf715e654 "Why peace talks are going on without Donald Trump﻿")

New Zealand Civil Defence Minister Mark Mitchell told _Stuff_ that the nation is prepared for Cyclone Vaianu.

﻿"We've seen it come through Fiji and the devastation it's caused there," he told _Stuff_.

"We know it's coming, it's on its way to us. We've learned the lessons from Cyclone Gabrielle, and the whole emergency management system is activated.

"We're as ready as we can to have the strongest response that we can deliver. Which, of course, helps with a strong recovery as well."
